intent: Check whether the candidate understands HTTP method semantics beyond CRUD naming, especially replacement versus partial update.
primaryQuestion: What are the main HTTP methods, and what is the difference between PUT and PATCH?

expectedSignals.label: Explains GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, and DELETE by intent and resource semantics.

requirements.description: GET reads a representation without requesting state change.
POST creates subordinate resources or triggers processing when semantics are not idempotent replacement.

expectedSignals.label: Distinguishes PUT as replacing a resource representation at a known URI.

requirements.description: PUT is used when the client sends the desired full representation or replacement state.
PUT to the same URI with the same representation should have the same intended effect.

expectedSignals.label: Distinguishes PATCH as partial modification of a resource.

requirements.description: PATCH sends a change document or partial fields rather than the whole resource.
PATCH semantics depend on the patch format and may or may not be idempotent by design.

expectedSignals.label: Connects method choice to validation, missing fields, and client/server contracts.

requirements.description: Explains why omitted fields in PUT and PATCH can mean different things.
Mentions documenting method behavior so clients do not accidentally erase fields.

scoringHints.description: Covers the mechanism, practical example, edge cases, and production consequence with clear reasoning.
Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.
Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.
Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

followUps.question: If PATCH /users/1 only sends { "name": "Ava" }, what should happen to email?
When would POST be a better fit than PUT for a create operation?
followUps.purpose: Probe resource identity and idempotency reasoning.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate can reason about retries, side effects, and caching from HTTP semantics.
primaryQuestion: What does safe, idempotent, and cacheable mean for HTTP methods, and why do these differences matter?

expectedSignals.label: Defines safe methods as methods whose requested semantics are read-only.

requirements.description: GET, HEAD, and OPTIONS are safe by intended semantics.
Safe does not mean no logging or metrics happen internally.

expectedSignals.label: Defines idempotency as same intended effect after one or multiple identical requests.

requirements.description: PUT and DELETE are idempotent by method semantics when implemented correctly.
POST is generally not idempotent unless an application-level idempotency key or equivalent is added.

expectedSignals.label: Separates cacheability from safety and idempotency.

requirements.description: Cacheability depends on method semantics and response caching headers.
A method can be idempotent without being useful to cache.

expectedSignals.label: Connects idempotency to retry safety in unreliable networks.

requirements.description: Explains why clients, proxies, or workers may retry requests after timeouts.
Mentions duplicate orders or payments as a risk when non-idempotent operations are retried.

scoringHints.description: Covers the mechanism, practical example, edge cases, and production consequence with clear reasoning.
Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.
Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.
Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

followUps.question: Is DELETE /users/1 idempotent if the second call returns 404? Explain the intended effect.

intent: Check whether the candidate can map API outcomes to precise HTTP responses rather than returning 200 for everything.
primaryQuestion: Explain common HTTP status codes such as 200, 201, 204, 400, 401, 403, 404, 409, 422, and 500. When should each be used?

expectedSignals.label: Explains successful responses accurately.

requirements.description: 200 is a successful response with a representation.
201 indicates a resource was created and may include Location or created representation.
204 indicates success with no response body.

expectedSignals.label: Distinguishes client input and validation errors.

requirements.description: 400 covers malformed request syntax or invalid request shape.
422 is useful when syntax is valid but semantic validation fails.
409 represents conflict with current resource state such as duplicate unique value or version conflict.

expectedSignals.label: Distinguishes authentication and authorization failures.

requirements.description: 401 means authentication is missing or invalid and a challenge may be appropriate.
403 means the authenticated principal is not allowed to perform the action.

expectedSignals.label: Uses 404 and 500 without hiding real server errors.

requirements.description: 404 means the target resource is not found or not exposed.
500 means unexpected server failure, not normal validation failure.

scoringHints.description: Covers the mechanism, practical example, edge cases, and production consequence with clear reasoning.
Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.
Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.
Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

intent: Assess whether the candidate understands REST as an architectural style, especially stateless request design and cacheable uniform interfaces.
primaryQuestion: What are the REST constraints, and what does stateless mean in a REST API?

expectedSignals.label: Names the core REST constraints at a high level.

requirements.description: Mentions client-server, stateless, cacheable, uniform interface, layered system, and optional code-on-demand.
Avoids reducing REST to only URL naming conventions.

expectedSignals.label: Explains stateless requests accurately.

requirements.description: Each request carries enough context for the server to understand it.
The server should not depend on hidden conversation state from previous requests to process the current request.

expectedSignals.label: Connects statelessness to scaling, reliability, and load balancing.

requirements.description: Explains why stateless APIs are easier to scale across instances.
Mentions that authentication can still use tokens or session identifiers if the request carries the necessary reference.

expectedSignals.label: Distinguishes REST constraints from dogmatic implementation rules.

requirements.description: Recognizes REST allows different representations and versioning strategies.
Explains that pragmatic APIs may be REST-like without perfectly satisfying every constraint.

scoringHints.description: Covers the mechanism, practical example, edge cases, and production consequence with clear reasoning.
Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.
Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.
Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

intent: Check whether the candidate separates identity verification from permission checks and maps failures to correct API behavior.
primaryQuestion: What is the difference between authentication and authorization, and how do they apply in a REST API?

expectedSignals.label: Defines authentication as proving who the caller is.

requirements.description: Mentions credentials, sessions, bearer tokens, or cookies as authentication mechanisms.
Explains that authentication establishes an identity or principal.

expectedSignals.label: Defines authorization as deciding what that identity can access or do.

requirements.description: Mentions roles, permissions, scopes, ownership checks, or policy checks.
Explains that authorization can fail even when authentication succeeds.

expectedSignals.label: Maps failures to practical REST responses.

requirements.description: 401 is appropriate for missing or invalid authentication.
403 is appropriate when the caller is authenticated but not allowed.

expectedSignals.label: Places checks at the correct API boundary.

requirements.description: Authentication usually happens before route business logic.
Authorization often uses route, resource, and user context such as owner ID or required scope.

scoringHints.description: Covers the mechanism, practical example, edge cases, and production consequence with clear reasoning.
Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.
Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.
Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate understands CORS as a browser security policy and server opt-in mechanism, not a generic backend networking error.
primaryQuestion: What is CORS, why does the browser enforce it, and what is a preflight request?

expectedSignals.label: Defines CORS as browser-controlled cross-origin access using response headers.

requirements.description: Mentions that browsers enforce same-origin policy and CORS relaxes it by server opt-in.
Mentions Access-Control-Allow-Origin or related CORS response headers.

expectedSignals.label: Explains preflight as an OPTIONS request for non-simple cross-origin requests.

requirements.description: Preflight checks whether the server allows the method and headers before the real request.
Mentions Access-Control-Allow-Methods and Access-Control-Allow-Headers.

expectedSignals.label: Avoids confusing CORS with server-to-server requests.

requirements.description: Recognizes CORS is mainly a browser enforcement issue.
Explains that Postman or backend-to-backend calls may not show the same CORS block.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions safe credential configuration.

requirements.description: Explains that credentials require explicit origin handling rather than wildcard origin.
Mentions cookies or Authorization headers as cases needing careful CORS configuration.

scoringHints.description: Covers the mechanism, practical example, edge cases, and production consequence with clear reasoning.
Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.
Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.

intent: Check whether the candidate can reason about metadata, representation formats, auth, caching, and content negotiation.
primaryQuestion: What information is carried in HTTP request headers versus response headers, and how do Accept and Content-Type differ?

expectedSignals.label: Separates request metadata from response metadata.

requirements.description: Request headers describe client preferences, credentials, body format, and conditional request information.
Response headers describe server metadata such as content type, caching, cookies, or rate-limit information.

expectedSignals.label: Distinguishes Content-Type from Accept.

requirements.description: Content-Type describes the media type of the message body being sent.
Accept describes media types the client is willing to receive.

expectedSignals.label: Gives practical examples of common headers.

requirements.description: Authorization carries credentials such as bearer tokens.
Cache-Control influences caching behavior.
ETag can support conditional requests.

expectedSignals.label: Connects incorrect headers to real bugs.

requirements.description: Wrong Content-Type can break body parsing or validation.
Missing Authorization or wrong Accept behavior can lead to 401, 415, or 406-style issues depending on API design.

scoringHints.description: Covers the mechanism, practical example, edge cases, and production consequence with clear reasoning.
Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.
Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.
Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

intent: Assess practical endpoint design for resources, identifiers, filtering, and request payload boundaries.
primaryQuestion: What makes an endpoint RESTful? Explain resource-based URLs, nouns versus verbs, and path parameters, query parameters, and request body usage.

expectedSignals.label: Uses resource nouns and HTTP methods rather than action-heavy URLs.

requirements.description: Names resources such as /users or /orders instead of /getUsers for normal CRUD-style APIs.
Uses method semantics to express read, create, replace, update, or delete.

expectedSignals.label: Uses path parameters for resource identity.

requirements.description: Explains that /users/{id} identifies a specific user resource.
Avoids putting stable resource IDs only in request bodies for ordinary resource URLs.

expectedSignals.label: Uses query parameters for collection controls.

requirements.description: Explains filtering, sorting, searching, pagination, and include options as query parameters.
Distinguishes query parameters from identity path segments.

expectedSignals.label: Uses request body for representation or command payload where appropriate.

requirements.description: Explains that POST, PUT, and PATCH often carry JSON bodies.
Avoids sending sensitive or large payloads in URLs.

scoringHints.description: Covers the mechanism, practical example, edge cases, and production consequence with clear reasoning.
Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.
Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.
Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

intent: Check whether the candidate can model long-running or queued work without lying that processing has already completed.
primaryQuestion: When should an API return 202 Accepted instead of 200 OK or 201 Created?

expectedSignals.label: Explains that 202 means the request was accepted for processing but not completed yet.

requirements.description: Separates accepted from completed success.
Avoids returning 201 when no resource is actually created yet.

expectedSignals.label: Gives realistic use cases for queued or long-running work.

requirements.description: Mentions imports, report generation, background jobs, email sending, video processing, or payment review.
Explains why synchronous 200 may be misleading or too slow.

expectedSignals.label: Describes how clients should track completion.

requirements.description: Mentions returning a job ID, status URL, Location header, polling endpoint, webhook, or callback mechanism.
Defines possible states such as pending, processing, succeeded, and failed.

expectedSignals.label: Handles error and retry implications.

requirements.description: Explains that later job failure must be observable to the client.
Mentions idempotency or duplicate submission protection for retryable accepted requests.

Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.

Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

expectedSignals.label: Includes observability metadata for support.

requirements.description: Mentions request ID, trace ID, timestamp, or documentation link.
Connects response format to logging and debugging across services.

intent: Check whether the candidate understands API pagination trade-offs for performance and consistency as datasets grow.
primaryQuestion: How do you implement pagination in a REST API, and how do offset-based and cursor-based pagination compare?

expectedSignals.label: Explains offset pagination mechanics and benefits.

requirements.description: Uses limit and offset or page and pageSize parameters.
Mentions simplicity and easy random page access.

expectedSignals.label: Explains offset drawbacks on large or changing datasets.

requirements.description: Large offsets can become slow because many rows are skipped.
Concurrent inserts or deletes can cause duplicates or missing items across pages.

expectedSignals.label: Explains cursor pagination mechanics and benefits.

requirements.description: Uses a stable cursor based on sort key, ID, timestamp, or encoded position.
Improves consistency and performance for forward scrolling over large datasets.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions response metadata and stable ordering.

requirements.description: Includes nextCursor, hasMore, total count when appropriate, or links.
Explains that pagination needs deterministic sort order.

scoringHints.description: Covers the mechanism, practical example, edge cases, and production consequence with clear reasoning.
Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.
Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.
Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

intent: Assess whether the candidate can design predictable collection query parameters with validation, indexing awareness, and response stability.

expectedSignals.label: Uses query parameters for collection controls.

requirements.description: Places filters such as status=active or role=admin in query parameters.
Uses sort parameters with documented allowed fields and direction.

expectedSignals.label: Validates and whitelists query fields.

requirements.description: Rejects or ignores unknown filter and sort fields intentionally.
Avoids directly interpolating query parameters into SQL.

expectedSignals.label: Distinguishes filtering from full-text search.

requirements.description: Explains exact filters versus free-text search query q.
Mentions search relevance, indexes, or dedicated search backend when needed.

expectedSignals.label: Maintains pagination and deterministic ordering.

requirements.description: Combines filters and sort with pagination metadata.
Uses tie-breaker ordering to avoid unstable pages.

Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.
Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

intent: Check whether the candidate understands API compatibility, breaking changes, and practical versioning trade-offs.
primaryQuestion: What is API versioning? Compare URL versioning with header-based versioning.

expectedSignals.label: Defines versioning as managing API contract evolution.

requirements.description: Connects versioning to breaking changes in request or response contracts.
Distinguishes versioning from ordinary additive backward-compatible changes.

expectedSignals.label: Explains URL versioning benefits and drawbacks.

requirements.description: URL versioning such as /v1/users is visible, cache-friendly, and easy to route.
It can clutter URLs and encourage coarse-grained versions.

expectedSignals.label: Explains header or media-type versioning benefits and drawbacks.

requirements.description: Header versioning keeps resource URLs stable.
It can be less visible and harder to test manually or cache depending on infrastructure.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions migration and deprecation practices.

requirements.description: Documents supported versions and deprecation timelines.
Keeps old clients working while new clients adopt the changed contract.

scoringHints.description: Covers the mechanism, practical example, edge cases, and production consequence with clear reasoning.
Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.
Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.
Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate can reason about freshness, validation, bandwidth reduction, and safe caching boundaries.
primaryQuestion: How does HTTP caching work? Explain Cache-Control, ETag, and conditional requests.

expectedSignals.label: Explains Cache-Control as freshness and storage instructions.

requirements.description: Mentions directives such as max-age, no-store, no-cache, private, or public at a high level.
Connects caching directives to browsers, CDNs, or intermediaries.

expectedSignals.label: Explains ETag as a validator for a representation.

requirements.description: ETag changes when the representation changes.
Clients can send If-None-Match to validate cached content.

expectedSignals.label: Explains conditional response behavior such as 304 Not Modified.

requirements.description: 304 tells the client cached representation is still valid.
Conditional requests can save bandwidth and server work.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions risks for personalized or sensitive data.

requirements.description: Avoids caching private user data in shared caches by accident.
Mentions using no-store or private where appropriate.

scoringHints.description: Covers the mechanism, practical example, edge cases, and production consequence with clear reasoning.
Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.
Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.
Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

guidance: 定義だけでなく、中心となる仕組みから説明する。
その概念が挙動を変える具体的な backend/frontend の例を使う。
よくある mistake または production consequence を一つ述べる。
commonMistakes: 暗記した keyword だけで答える。
boundary case や failure mode を無視する。
概念を実装上の判断につなげない。

sourceReferences.label: RFC 9110: HTTP Semantics
sourceReferences.note: Canonical reference for HTTP methods, status codes, semantics, caching, and content negotiation.

sourceReferences.label: MDN HTTP
sourceReferences.note: Used for practical HTTP headers, CORS, caching, and browser behavior.

sourceReferences.label: OpenAPI Specification

intent: Check whether the candidate can prevent duplicate side effects caused by retries, double-clicks, and network timeouts.
primaryQuestion: What is an idempotency key, and why is it useful for payment or order creation endpoints?

expectedSignals.label: Defines an idempotency key as a client-provided unique key for one logical operation.

requirements.description: The server stores the key with request outcome or operation identity.
Repeated requests with the same key return the same result or do not duplicate the side effect.

expectedSignals.label: Explains retry and timeout scenarios.

requirements.description: Client may not know whether the first request succeeded after a timeout.
Retrying without protection can create duplicate orders, charges, or emails.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions server-side storage and comparison rules.

requirements.description: Stores key with user/account scope and expiration window.
Rejects or handles same key with different payload to avoid accidental key reuse.

expectedSignals.label: Connects to method semantics and response behavior.

requirements.description: Explains why POST create endpoints often need application-level idempotency.
Mentions returning the original response or operation status on retry.

scoringHints.description: Covers the mechanism, practical example, edge cases, and production consequence with clear reasoning.
Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.
Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.
Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

followUps.question: What should the server do if the same idempotency key is retried after a payment timeout?
Why should the key usually be scoped to the user or merchant?

en.title: Idempotency keys for duplicate prevention
en.displayQuestion: What is an idempotency key, and why is it useful for payment or order creation endpoints?
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate can prevent duplicate side effects caused by retries, double-clicks, and network timeouts.
guidance: Start with the core mechanism, not only a definition.
Use a concrete backend or frontend example where the concept changes behavior.
Name one common mistake or production consequence.
commonMistakes: Answering only with memorized keywords.
Ignoring boundary cases or failure modes.
Not connecting the concept to real implementation decisions.

vi.title: Idempotency key để chống duplicate
vi.displayQuestion: Idempotency key là gì, và vì sao nó hữu ích cho endpoint tạo payment hoặc order?
vi.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate can prevent duplicate side effects caused by retries, double-clicks, and network timeouts.
guidance: Bắt đầu bằng cơ chế cốt lõi, không chỉ đọc định nghĩa.
Dùng một ví dụ backend hoặc frontend cụ thể nơi khái niệm này làm thay đổi hành vi.
Nêu một lỗi phổ biến hoặc hệ quả trong production.
commonMistakes: Chỉ trả lời bằng keyword học thuộc.
Bỏ qua boundary case hoặc failure mode.
Không kết nối khái niệm với quyết định implement thực tế.
